data.content:
Now I have the full picture. Let me create the `Directory.Packages.props` and strip versions from all csproj files.

**Version conflicts resolved (highest wins):**
- `System.Text.Json`: 10.0.1 (was 8.0.4 in Worker)
- `Azure.Identity`: 1.13.2 (was 1.11.4 in Shared)

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Azure.Identity" Version="1.13.2" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting" Version="1.15.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="10.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.9.3" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I need to identify all packages and their versions, then pick the highest version for each:

| Package | Api | Worker | Shared | Tests | Highest |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| System.Text.Json | 10.0.1 | 8.0.4 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 | 10.0.1 |
| Azure.Identity | 1.13.2 | - | 1.11.4 | - | 1.13.2 |
| Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting | - | 8.0.1 | - | - | 8.0.1 |


| OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting | - | 1.15.0 | - | - | 1.15.0 |
| xunit | - | - | - | 2.9.3 | 2.9.3 |

Now I'll create a centralized package management file at the solution root with all the highest versions, then strip the version attributes from each project's package references.
